content.full_text:
State of California under restricted circumstances.

Memorandum

To : Opinion Request Meeting Participants                  Date : May 3, 1977

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
       Del

Subject: Palo Verde Irrigation District Conflict of Interest Code

     As the attached letter indicates, the attorney for
the Palo Verde Irrigation District desires to add language
to the disqualification section of the Code for the District to provide that the fixing of uniform water tolls
would not be the subject of disqualification for District
directors who own real property subject to such tolls.

     I would prefer to handle this matter in the context
of an advice letter construing the present language of
the Code, rather than as an amendment to the Code, which
was approved by the Commission pursuant to its summary
calendar.

     I see no real problem in providing the kind of advice
which attorney Mann seeks, except that I would caveat the
advice with the provision that it does not include the
determination to raise monies via water tolls versus ad
valorem taxes. In other words, so long as the percentage
of water tolls versus property tax ratio remains relatively
the same, there is no potential for conflict. If, however,
the water toll rate remains the same while the property tax
rate rises by virtue of a District decision, the potential
for conflict would be present.


DLS:glb
attachment

Deis proposal was accepted on May 13, 1977.

CLAYSON, ROTHROCK & MANN
ATTORNEYS AT LAW
CLAYSON LAW BUILDING
6500 MAIN STREET
POST OFFICE BOX 570
CORONA, CALIFORNIA 91720

WALTER S. CLAYSON
1883-1972

OF COUNSEL
DONALD O. STARK

AREA CODE 714
737-1910
539-7224

April 22, 1977


Mr. Delbert L. Spurlock, Jr.
Chief, Conflicts of Interest Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Conflicts of Interest Code
    Palo Verde Irrigation District

Dear Mr. Spurlock:

    At our meeting in your office on April 8, we
discussed several problems the PVID board of trustees was
having with the conflicts of interest code proposed by the
Commission. You suggested some possible changes in language
which we felt would clarify the meaning in some areas.

    I have now had the opportunity to review our
discussion with the PVID board and they have asked me to
submit the enclosed draft.

    The base document is the Code as received from
you with your letter of February 7, 1977. All changes have
been marked in yellow. I believe we discussed all of them
and you felt they would all be acceptable except for the
insertion of water tolls in Paragraph D2 on Page 7.

    I have tried to make this acceptable by making it
a "uniform water toll". This is particularly important
because the PVID raises more than 90% of its funds from
water tolls paid by landowners. The board of trustees is
normally composed entirely of landowners who pay water tolls.
The City of Blythe is in the District, however. Residential
lots do not pay water tolls (with certain minor exceptions
for large lots which use irrigation water). The PVID only
serves irrigation water and does drainage and flood protection

Mr. Delbert L. Spurlock, Jr.
April 22, 1977
Page 2

work. The result is that fewer parcels pay water tolls
than do not (although a far greater acreage pays water
toll than does not). Residential lots contributes a
negligible amount of the revenues and no water tolls.
Fewer people in the District pay water tolls than do not.

Under the circumstances, this code should provide
that a trustee is not disqualified from setting water tolls
because he will have to pay them. With this District the
setting of water tolls is virtually indistinguishable from
ad valorem taxes. We do not want to leave the code open to
the possible interpretation that the members of the board,
if any, who do not pay water tolls have the only power to
set them.

If these proposed changes are acceptable, the
PVID board feels a hearing would not be necessary. If
they are not, I would appreciate a chance to discuss them
with you.

Yours very truly,

Roy H. Mann, Attorney
for/Palo Verde Irrigation
District

RHM;bh
Enclosure
cc: Virgil Jones
Dana Fisher

sections.question: Whether adding water tolls to the Palo Verde Irrigation District Conflict of Interest Code would create disqualification issues for board members, and whether the proposed language adequately addresses potential conflicts of interest related to water toll decisions.
sections.conclusion: The FPPC found no real conflict of interest problem with the proposed addition of water tolls to the code, provided the toll rate structure remains distinct from ad valorem tax rates. However, the FPPC expressed concern about leaving the code open to interpretation that board members who do not pay water tolls could have power to set them, and recommended against such language to avoid potential conflicts.

sections.question_synthetic: The Palo Verde Irrigation District sought FPPC guidance on whether adding water tolls to its Conflict of Interest Code would disqualify directors who are subject to such tolls, and whether the proposed code language adequately protects against conflicts of interest in water toll-setting decisions.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC approved the proposed addition of water tolls to the Conflict of Interest Code with the caveat that water toll decisions must remain distinguishable from ad valorem tax decisions, and the code language must not permit board members exempt from water tolls to unilaterally set toll rates.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: old; LLM: Document is heavily degraded with significant scanning/OCR errors making some sections difficult to read with certainty. The core Q/C structure is identifiable but portions of the reasoning are obscured. Letter dated April 22, 1977, from FPPC to Palo Verde Irrigation District regarding conflict of interest code amendments.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether adding water tolls to the Palo Verde Irrigation District Conflict of Interest Code would create disqualification issues for board members, and whether the proposed language adequately addresses potential conflicts of interest related to water toll decisions.

CONCLUSION: The FPPC found no real conflict of interest problem with the proposed addition of water tolls to the code, provided the toll rate structure remains distinct from ad valorem tax rates. However, the FPPC expressed concern about leaving the code open to interpretation that board members who do not pay water tolls could have power to set them, and recommended against such language to avoid potential conflicts.

I would prefer to handle this matter in the context of an advice letter construing the present language of the Code, rather than as an amendment to the Code, which was approved by the Commission pursuant to its summary calendar. I see no real problem in providing the kind of advice which attorney Mann seeks, except that I would caveat the advice with the provision that it does not include the determination to raise monies via water tolls versus ad valorem taxes. In other words, so long as the percentage of water tolls versus property tax ratio remains relatively the same, there is no potential for conflict. If, however, the water toll rate remains the same while the property tax rate rises by virtue of a District decision, the potential for conflict would be present. embedding.summary: In response to the Palo Verde Irrigation District's request, the FPPC provided advice on incorporating water tolls into the district's Conflict of Interest Code. The FPPC found the proposed amendments acceptable but cautioned that the language must clearly prevent any interpretation allowing board members not subject to water tolls to control toll-setting decisions.
